title: UConn vs Illinois — Final Four Recap
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Final Four • Indianapolis, IN
venue: Lucas Oil Stadium, Indianapolis, Indiana • TBS
extraInfo: FINAL: UConn 71, Illinois 62. UConn advances to the National Championship Game on Monday, April 6 vs. Michigan

stakes.callout: FINAL: UConn 71, Illinois 62. Dan Hurley's Huskies muscled their way past Illinois to reach their third national championship game in four years — something no program has done since UCLA in the 1970s. Tarris Reed Jr. dominated with 17 points and 11 rebounds, Braylon Mullins delivered another clutch three with 52 seconds left, and UConn committed just 4 turnovers while holding Illinois to 33.9% shooting. Brad Underwood's 21-year Final Four drought ended — but so did Illinois's season, with an emotional Underwood fighting back tears: "I feel sad. I'm sad. If you want to know the truth, I am sad."

cards.label: UConn Advances
cards.value: Dan Hurley is in the national championship game for the third time in four years. UConn (34-5) will face Michigan on Monday night. The Huskies extended their Sweet 16+ NCAA Tournament winning streak to 19 consecutive games. A win would give them their seventh national title and match UCLA's feat of three championships in four years (1975). Hurley after the win: "I just couldn't be more proud of my guys and how hard they fought when most people probably didn't think we were going to win."

cards.label: Illinois Falls Short
cards.value: Illinois's historic season ends at 28-9. The first Final Four since 2005 ends with UConn — again. Underwood's team shot their second-worst percentage of the season, managed a season-low 3 assists, and recorded zero fast-break points. Keaton Wagler led all scorers with 20 points but shot just 2-of-10 from three. Underwood, near tears: "When they beat us in the Elite Eight, we were right there. That was a bad feeling. This is even worse."

cards.label: The Mullins Dagger
cards.value: Braylon Mullins went 0-for-5 in the second half before hitting the shot that mattered most — a catch-and-shoot three-pointer with 52 seconds left to push UConn's lead to 66-59. Keaton Wagler answered with a three just 8 seconds later (66-62), but it was too late. Without Mullins' shot, the game could have come down to a single possession. Teammate Jaylin Stewart predicted it from the bench: "I'm calling a Braylon Mullins 3-pointer right here."

cards.label: The Injury Toll
cards.value: Solo Ball, playing through a mid-December wrist injury that had cratered his three-point shooting from 41% to 29%, had his best perimeter game in weeks (3-of-7 from three, 13 points) — then suffered a left foot sprain and was placed in a walking boot postgame. Silas Demary Jr. appeared healthy (31 minutes, 9 rebounds, 7 assists) after his ankle injury. Jaylin Stewart played just 7 minutes in his continued return from knee injury.

styles.title: How UConn Won: Defense, Ball Control, and Clutch Shooting
bullets: Defensive suffocation: UConn held the #1 KenPom offense in history to just 33.9% shooting — Illinois's second-worst of the season. The Illini managed a season-low 3 assists and zero fast-break points. UConn's half-court defense forced Illinois into contested jumpers and limited the Illini's normally potent ball movement to a standstill.

Zero first-half turnovers: UConn committed zero turnovers before halftime — a record under Dan Hurley. They tallied 9 assists to Illinois's 1 in the first half, and finished with just 4 turnovers for the entire game compared to Illinois's 8. The Huskies' ball security denied Illinois any transition opportunities.

Reed dominated inside again: Tarris Reed Jr. finished with 17 points (6-12 FG, 5-5 FT) and 11 rebounds — his fifth dominant performance in five tournament games. He continued making his case as the tournament's most impactful player, controlling the paint against Illinois's twin towers.

Three-point shooting was the difference: UConn hit 12-of-33 from three (36.4%) — the most threes in a Final Four game in program history. Illinois shot just 6-of-26 (23.1%). That 13-percentage-point gap was the game's decisive margin.

Demary's near triple-double: Silas Demary Jr. was the quiet engine — 7 points, 9 rebounds, 7 assists, 2 steals, zero turnovers in 31 minutes. He appeared fully healthy after weeks of managing his ankle injury and controlled the game without needing to score.

styles.title: How Illinois Lost: Cold Shooting, No Transition, Late Rally Falls Short
bullets: The three-point shot abandoned them: Illinois shot 6-of-26 from three (23.1%), including just 3-of-14 in the first half. Keaton Wagler — the South Region MOP who shoots 40.8% — went 2-of-10 from deep. Ben Humrichous was 1-of-4, Tomislav Ivisic 2-of-7. Without the three-ball, Illinois couldn't space the floor against UConn's pack defense.

The rebounding advantage didn't translate: Illinois outrebounded UConn 44-37 — normally a formula for victory. But the Illini shot so poorly that extra possessions didn't generate enough points. They scored on only 33.9% of their field goal attempts, and second chances couldn't overcome the efficiency gap.

No transition game: Illinois recorded zero fast-break points for the game. UConn's 4 turnovers and disciplined half-court play completely eliminated Illinois's ability to run, which had been a weapon in their four double-digit tournament wins.

The rally came up short: Down 57-43 midway through the second half, Illinois launched an 8-0 run to cut it to 57-51. They got within four on multiple occasions late, but UConn answered every time. Mullins' dagger three with 52 seconds left (66-59) was the final blow, even though Wagler hit a three 8 seconds later.

Key contributors went silent: David Mirkovic — who had 29/17 against Penn — scored just 6 points on 2-of-7 shooting. Kylan Boswell was 1-of-6 for 6 points. Andrej Stojakovic shot 4-of-10 with 4 missed free throws. Only Wagler and Tomislav Ivisic produced consistently.

keys.title: UConn's ball security strangled Illinois's transition game.
keys.description: UConn committed zero turnovers in the first half — a record under Dan Hurley — and finished with just 4 for the game. Illinois thrives on turning opponents over and running in transition, but they recorded zero fast-break points. UConn's 9 first-half assists to Illinois's 1 told the story: the Huskies controlled tempo, ran their offense, and never gave Illinois a chance to play their up-tempo style.

keys.title: The three-point gap was the decisive margin.
keys.description: UConn hit 12-of-33 from three (36.4%) — the most in a Final Four game in program history. Illinois shot just 6-of-26 (23.1%). Wagler went 2-of-10, Humrichous 1-of-4, Tomislav Ivisic 2-of-7. The 13-percentage-point three-point differential accounted for the entire scoring gap. When both teams went to the Elite Eight shooting cold from deep, the question was which team would find its stroke first — UConn answered.

keys.title: Reed won the frontcourt battle against the twin towers.
keys.description: Tarris Reed Jr. posted 17 points and 11 rebounds on 6-of-12 shooting and went 5-for-5 from the line against Illinois's twin 7-footers. Tomislav Ivisic answered with 16 points but on just 4-of-11 shooting. Zvonimir Ivisic was invisible — 0 points on 0-of-4 shooting in 13 minutes. Reed's ability to draw fouls and convert at the line (after shooting .561 on the season) was critical — he didn't miss a single free throw.

keys.title: Mullins delivered the dagger — again.
keys.description: After going 0-for-5 in the second half, Braylon Mullins hit a catch-and-shoot three-pointer with 52 seconds left to push UConn's lead to 66-59. Wagler answered with a three 8 seconds later (66-62), but without Mullins' shot, the game could have been a one-point affair. This followed his 35-foot buzzer-beater to beat Duke in the Elite Eight. Teammate Jaylin Stewart called it from the bench: "I'm calling a Braylon Mullins 3-pointer right here."

keys.title: Illinois's late rally proved they belonged — but UConn's experience closed it.
keys.description: Down 57-43, Illinois ripped off an 8-0 run to cut it to 57-51. They got within four on multiple occasions. But UConn answered every time. Karaban went 6-of-6 from the free throw line. Demary controlled the ball with 7 assists and zero turnovers. Illinois's 0-5 record in games decided by 3 or fewer this season hinted at this outcome — the Illini could rally but couldn't close. Underwood: "Winning is really hard."

prediction.summary:
FINAL: UConn 71, Illinois 62. UConn's championship pedigree and elite defense proved the difference against Illinois's historically great offense. Both teams shot poorly from the field — UConn 35.5%, Illinois 33.9% — but the Huskies hit 12 three-pointers (36.4%) to Illinois's 6 (23.1%), a gap that decided the game. UConn committed just 4 turnovers (zero in the first half) while Illinois managed a season-low 3 assists and zero fast-break points. Tarris Reed Jr. dominated inside with 17 and 11, Mullins hit the dagger three with 52 seconds left, and Demary controlled the tempo with 7 assists and zero turnovers.

Illinois outrebounded UConn 44-37 and cut a 14-point deficit to 4 on multiple occasions late, but could never get over the hump. The Illini's 0-5 record in games decided by 3 or fewer proved prophetic — they could rally but couldn't close against a team with 19 straight Sweet 16+ tournament wins. Underwood, near tears: "Winning is really hard." Hurley, headbutting Mullins: "We're a tough program, we're a group of fighters."

phases.title: First Half — UConn establishes control (UConn 37, Illinois 29)
phases.description: UConn jumped out to a 10-7 lead at the first media timeout. Illinois briefly took their only lead at 22-21 on a Tomislav Ivisic three. Then UConn closed the half on a 16-7 run, with Mullins (12 pts) and Reed (11 pts, 5 reb) dominating. The Huskies committed zero turnovers and had 9 assists to Illinois's 1. Illinois shot 3-of-14 from three in the half.

phases.title: Early Second Half — UConn extends to 14 (UConn 57, Illinois 43)
phases.description: UConn pushed the lead to its largest margin of 14 points at 57-43 midway through the second half. Solo Ball hit three-pointers despite his wrist injury, and the defense continued to stifle Illinois's ball movement. The KenPom #1 offense looked ordinary against UConn's half-court defense.

phases.title: Illinois Rally — The deficit shrinks to 4 (UConn 61, Illinois 57)
phases.description: Illinois ripped off an 8-0 run to cut it to 57-51 with 6:08 remaining. Ben Humrichous hit a three to make it 61-56. The Illini got within four on multiple occasions, and the Illini-heavy crowd roared. UConn called timeout to steady themselves.

phases.title: Final 2 Minutes — Mullins delivers the dagger (UConn 71, Illinois 62)
phases.description: With 52 seconds left and UConn clinging to a 63-59 lead, Mullins — 0-for-5 in the second half — hit a catch-and-shoot three to make it 66-59. Wagler answered with a three 8 seconds later (66-62). Underwood burned his final timeout with 43 seconds left, but UConn converted 5-of-6 free throws in the final minute to seal it 71-62.

projectedScore.rationale: UConn's defensive identity, ball security (4 turnovers), and three-point shooting (36.4%) overcame Illinois's rebounding advantage (44-37). Reed's 17/11 double-double and Mullins' dagger three sealed it. Illinois's cold shooting (33.9% FG, 23.1% 3PT) and season-low 3 assists told the story of a team that couldn't execute against championship-caliber defense.

players.name: Tarris Reed Jr.
players.stats: 17 PTS (6-12 FG, 5-5 FT), 11 REB | East Region MOP
players.bio:
The East Region Most Outstanding Player delivered another dominant performance with a 17-point, 11-rebound double-double against Illinois's twin 7-footers. Shot 6-of-12 from the field and went perfect from the line (5-5) — remarkable for a .561 free throw shooter on the season. Through five tournament games, Reed has been the most dominant big man in March, including a 31/27 game against Furman and 26/9 with 4 blocks against Duke. Transferred from Michigan after two seasons.

Andscape feature on Reed's tournament dominance

players.name: Braylon Mullins
players.bio: Led UConn with 12 first-half points, then went 0-for-5 in the second half — until the shot that mattered. With 52 seconds left and UConn up 63-59, Mullins hit a catch-and-shoot three to push it to 66-59. Jaylin Stewart called it from the bench: "I'm calling a Braylon Mullins 3-pointer right here." This was his second tournament-defining shot after the 35-foot buzzer-beater to beat Duke. From Greenfield, Indiana — 25 miles from Lucas Oil Stadium. Mullins and Wagler became the first freshman duo to both score 15+ in a Final Four since Michael Jordan and Patrick Ewing in 1982.

players.name: Silas Demary Jr.
players.stats: 7 PTS, 9 REB, 7 AST, 2 STL, 0 TO | Floor general
players.bio:
The quiet engine of UConn's win — a near triple-double with 7 points, 9 rebounds, 7 assists, 2 steals, and zero turnovers in 31 minutes. Appeared fully healthy after weeks of managing a high ankle sprain suffered in the Big East Tournament. His ball security was critical to UConn's 4-turnover game and zero first-half turnovers. Against Duke, he hit back-to-back threes that fueled the comeback and deflected the pass that led to Mullins' game-winner.

ESPN on Demary's recovery

players.name: Solo Ball
players.bio:
Had his best three-point shooting game in weeks — 3-of-7 from deep for 13 points — despite playing through a mid-December wrist injury that had dropped his three-point percentage from 41% to 29%. Then suffered a left foot sprain during the game and was placed in a walking boot postgame. His status for the championship game against Michigan was uncertain. Ball reached double figures for three consecutive tournament games after a rocky March stretch.

CBS Sports on Ball's wrist injury

players.name: Keaton Wagler
players.stats: 20 PTS (7-16 FG, 2-10 3PT), 8 REB | Led all scorers
players.bio:
Led all scorers with 20 points and 8 rebounds but shot just 2-of-10 from three — a night where UConn's defense limited the South Region MOP's effectiveness from deep. Still found ways to score, going 7-of-16 from the field and 4-of-5 from the line. Through five tournament games, Wagler scored 90 total points. Was 5-8, 110 lbs as a high school freshman — grew to 6-6, wasn't a top-100 recruit, and is now a projected top-6 NBA pick. Underwood: "He's as stoic, emotionless, and yet just that silent killer."

ESPN: The Rise of Keaton Wagler

players.name: Tomislav Ivisic
players.bio: Illinois's second-leading scorer with 16 points and 7 rebounds, including the three-pointer that gave Illinois its only lead at 22-21. Shot 4-of-11 from the field and was perfect from the line (6-6). His twin brother Zvonimir (7-2) was held scoreless in 13 minutes (0-of-4). The Ivisic twins give Underwood the rare ability to play two 7-footers simultaneously, but Reed's physicality was too much on this night.

players.name: Andrej Stojakovic
players.stats: 9 PTS (4-10 FG, 1-4 FT), 8 REB | Off the bench
players.bio: Son of NBA legend Peja Stojakovic. Had a quiet night compared to his Elite Eight explosion (17 on 7-9 FG) — finished with 9 points on 4-of-10 shooting and missed 3 of 4 free throws. Grabbed 8 rebounds in 29 minutes off the bench. Had been one of the tournament's best bench scorers (21 vs. VCU, 13 vs. Houston, 17 vs. Iowa) but couldn't replicate that against UConn's defense.

players.name: David Mirkovic
players.stats: 6 PTS (2-7 FG), 5 REB, 3 BLK | Quiet night
players.bio:
The tournament's most dominant rebounder had a subdued Final Four — just 6 points on 2-of-7 shooting and 5 rebounds, a far cry from his 29-point, 17-rebound explosion against Penn in the first round. Did contribute 3 blocks defensively. Left home in Montenegro at age 14 to pursue basketball. His double-double average in the first four tournament games couldn't continue against UConn's physicality.

Chicago Tribune feature on Mirkovic

funFacts.title: The 30-0 Ghost
funFacts.body: In the 2024 Elite Eight, UConn beat Illinois 77-52, including a 30-0 scoring run — the longest unanswered run in NCAA Tournament history at that stage. UConn went on to win back-to-back titles. Illinois used that humiliation as fuel to rebuild and return to the Final Four two years later.

funFacts.title: The International Illini
funFacts.body: Illinois's roster includes players from Montenegro (Mirkovic), Croatia (both Ivisic twins), and Serbia (via Sacramento — Stojakovic). The Ivisic twins — Tomislav from Vodice, Croatia, and Zvonimir from Vitez, Bosnia — are separated by four minutes of birth. Zvonimir played at Kentucky and Arkansas before reuniting with his twin at Illinois.

funFacts.title: 5-8, 110 to Lottery Pick
funFacts.body: Keaton Wagler was 5-foot-8 and 110 pounds as a high school freshman. He wasn't a top-100 national recruit. He played for an independent AAU program, not a shoe brand team. Illinois discovered him through staff connections. Now he's 6-6, the Big Ten Freshman of the Year, South Region MOP, and a projected top-6 NBA Draft pick.

funFacts.title: The Hurley Family Business
funFacts.body: Dan Hurley's father Bob Hurley Sr. won over 1,000 games at St. Anthony High School in Jersey City. His brother Bobby Hurley was a two-time national champion at Duke (1991, 1992) and now coaches Arizona State. Dan played at Seton Hall. The family has combined for multiple thousands of basketball wins across three generations.

funFacts.title: Peja's Boy
funFacts.body: Andrej Stojakovic's father Peja Stojakovic was a 3x NBA All-Star who won a championship with the 2011 Dallas Mavericks. Peja is the all-time leading scorer for the Sacramento Kings. Andrej grew up in Sacramento, played at Stanford and Cal, then transferred to Illinois — where he's become one of the best sixth men in college basketball.

funFacts.title: Tech Titans and Internet Founders
funFacts.body: Illinois alumni include Marc Andreessen (co-creator of the Mosaic browser, Netscape co-founder), Max Levchin (PayPal co-founder), and Jawed Karim (YouTube co-founder). The university has 24 Nobel laureates among alumni, faculty, and researchers.

funFacts.title: Jonathan Goes to Antarctica
funFacts.body: UConn's Husky mascot is named after Jonathan Trumbull, Connecticut's last colonial governor. One Jonathan traveled to Antarctica with Admiral Byrd's Operation Highjump in 1946-47. Another ran onto the court at Madison Square Garden during UConn's first NCAA Tournament appearance in 1951.

funFacts.title: 17 Basketball Titles — Most in NCAA History
funFacts.body: UConn holds 6 men's and 11 women's basketball national championships — 17 combined, the most by any school in NCAA basketball history. In 2004, UConn became the only school to win both the men's and women's championships in the same year.

funFacts.title: The Mascot That Isn't
funFacts.body: Illinois retired Chief Illiniwek in 2007 after decades of protest from Indigenous organizations. The university has had no official mascot since. Students voted for the belted kingfisher bird in both 2020 and 2025 referendums, but the matter remains officially unresolved.

funFacts.title: Hurley Turned Down the Lakers
funFacts.body: In June 2024, Dan Hurley declined a "massive offer" from the Los Angeles Lakers to stay at UConn. LeBron James praised him publicly: "He's so DAMN GOOD!! Along with his staff. Super creative with their offense! Love it." Hurley chose to chase a three-peat instead.

funFacts.title: Underwood's 39-Year Journey
funFacts.body: Brad Underwood has coached at Dodge City Community College, Daytona Beach CC, Western Illinois (10 years as assistant), Hardin-Simmons, Stephen F. Austin, Oklahoma State, and Illinois. After reaching the Final Four, he teared up: "This is the little-kid dream and the Dodge City, Dayton, Western Illinois, Hardin-Simmons, the Stephen F. Austin — this is what you dream about at those places."

funFacts.title: The Mullins Shot
funFacts.body: Braylon Mullins' 35-foot buzzer-beater to beat #1 Duke is already being compared to Christian Laettner's 1992 shot against Kentucky and Kris Jenkins' 2016 championship winner. His McDonald's All-American teammate at UConn, associate head coach Kimani Young, said: "I shoot with him every day. When he let that go, I knew it was going through."

history.callout: UConn and Illinois met in the NCAA Tournament twice in three years — and UConn won both. In the 2024 Elite Eight, UConn destroyed Illinois 77-52, including an infamous 30-0 scoring run. In the 2026 Final Four, UConn won 71-62, with Underwood saying it was "even worse" than 2024. Both UConn victories propelled the Huskies to the national championship game.

timeline.text: UConn wins its first national championship, beating Duke 77-74 in the title game. Richard Hamilton scores 27.
UConn beats Duke 79-78 in the Final Four, then wins its second title. UConn also wins the women's title — the only school to win both in the same year.
Kemba Walker leads UConn on an improbable run to the program's third championship, winning five games in five days at the Big East Tournament before running through March Madness.
UConn wins its fourth title as a #7 seed — the lowest seed ever to win the championship. Shabazz Napier carries the Huskies past Kentucky in the final.
Dan Hurley's UConn team wins the fifth championship, beating San Diego State 76-59. Every tournament game won by double digits.
UConn goes back-to-back, beating Purdue 75-60 in the final. Average margin: 23.3 points — a tournament record. Illinois is beaten 77-52 in the Elite Eight (30-0 run).
UConn beats Illinois 71-62 in the Final Four — their 19th straight Sweet 16+ tournament win. Advances to face Michigan for the national championship.
The "Flyin' Illini" under Lou Henson go 31-5 and reach the Final Four before falling to Michigan on a last-second shot.

Illinois goes 37-2 — one of the greatest seasons ever. Deron Williams, Luther Head, and Dee Brown reach the championship game. North Carolina wins 75-70. It remains the program's closest approach to a title.
Brad Underwood reaches the Elite Eight for the first time — but UConn delivers a 30-0 run in a 77-52 demolition. The loss becomes a rallying cry.

After losing two first-round NBA picks, Underwood rebuilds with international talent and freshmen. Illinois wins four straight tournament games by double digits, reaches the Final Four for the first time in 21 years, but falls to UConn 71-62. Underwood, in tears: "This is even worse" than 2024.

subsections.title: The November Meeting
subsections.content: UConn beat Illinois 74-61 at Madison Square Garden on November 28, 2025. Solomon Ball led UConn; Kylan Boswell paced Illinois. Both teams have evolved dramatically since — Illinois moved Wagler to point guard after the loss, transforming their offensive ceiling. This November defeat was one of the inflection points that shaped Illinois's Final Four identity.
